prompt: I destructure a struct to move out one String field, and then try to use the whole struct in a println. The compiler says the value was partially moved.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `p`
 --> src/main.rs:10:22
  |
9  |     let name = p.name;
   |                ------ value partially moved here
10 |     println!("{:?}", (p.name, p.age));
   |                       ^^^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: Moving `p.name` out of `p` partially invalidates the struct: `p.age` is still valid but `p` as a whole is not. The idiomatic fix is to destructure the struct completely in one `let` binding, moving all fields simultaneously; the whole struct is consumed at that point and both fields are available through their own bindings.

prompt: I move one field out of a struct and then try to pass the entire struct to a function. The compiler rejects this.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `cfg`
 --> src/main.rs:14:10
  |
12 |     let _host = cfg.host;
   |                 -------- value partially moved here
14 |     show(&cfg);
   |          ^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: Once `cfg.host` is moved out, `cfg` is partially moved and can no longer be borrowed as a whole. The fix is to fully destructure `cfg` first, then pass the individual fields to the function. Alternatively, borrow `cfg.host` with `&cfg.host` if you don't need to consume it.

prompt: I move a String out of an enum variant in one match arm, and later the compiler says the variable is partially moved when I try to match it again.
error_message:
error[E0382]: use of partially moved value: `msg`
 --> src/main.rs:12:11
  |
8  |     if let Msg::Text(s) = msg {
   |                      - value partially moved here
12 |     match msg {
   |           ^^^ value used here after partial move
explanation: The `if let` pattern `Msg::Text(s)` moves the inner String out of `msg`, partially invalidating it. Using `ref s` in the pattern borrows the String instead of moving it, so `msg` remains fully valid for the subsequent `match` which can then move out of the variant.

prompt: I move a field out of a struct inside a match arm and then try to use another field of the same struct below. The compiler says the struct is partially moved.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `resp`
 --> src/main.rs:12:36
  |
8  |     let body = resp.body;
   |                --------- value partially moved here
12 |     println!("full: {} {}", resp.status, resp.body);
   |                                          ^^^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: After `resp.body` is moved out, `resp.body` is invalid while `resp.status` (a `Copy` type) can still be read. However the whole struct cannot be matched or borrowed as one unit. Destructuring in the `let` binding upfront consumes the struct atomically, giving independent bindings for both fields.

prompt: I try to move two fields out of a struct in separate statements, and the second move works but using the struct afterward fails.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `pair.first`
 --> src/main.rs:14:24
  |
11 |     let a = pair.first;
   |             ---------- value moved here
...
14 |     println!("{} {}", pair.first, pair.second);
   |                        ^^^^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: Both fields are moved out across two separate statements; after both moves, neither field remains in `pair`. The compiler tracks field-level move status but forbids using the whole struct or any moved field. Destructuring in one binding communicates intent clearly and handles both fields at once.

prompt: I move a field from a struct inside a function, return early, and later the compiler says the struct is partially moved even in the non-early-return path.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `job`
 --> src/main.rs:12:38
  |
10 |     let p = job.payload;
   |             ----------- value partially moved here
12 |     println!("processing job {}", job.id);
   |                                   ^^^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: After `job.payload` is moved into `p`, `job` is partially moved: `job.id` is a `Copy` field so it can still be read, but the struct is no longer whole. Destructuring immediately after the guard communicates intent clearly and gives independent bindings for all fields, eliminating partial-move confusion.

prompt: I have a tuple and move the second element out. Later I try to use the whole tuple and get a partial move error.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `tup.1`
 --> src/main.rs:5:28
  |
3  |     let second = tup.1;
   |                  ----- value moved here
5  |     println!("{} {}", tup.0, tup.1);
   |                               ^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: Tuples, like structs, support field-level move tracking. Moving `tup.1` out of the tuple invalidates that slot. Destructuring the tuple in one binding — `let (first, second) = tup;` — cleanly moves both elements at once and provides separate bindings for each.

prompt: I implement a consuming method on a struct that moves one field but then tries to return `self`. The compiler says self was partially moved.
error_message:
error[E0382]: use of partially moved value: `self`
 --> src/main.rs:10:13
  |
9  |         let v = self.value;
   |                 ---------- value partially moved here
10 |         (v, self)
   |             ^^^^ value used here after partial move
explanation: After `self.value` is moved into `v`, the original `self` is partially moved and cannot be returned as a whole. The fix is to destructure `self` by returning each field individually rather than returning `self`. The tuple `(self.value, self.next)` moves both fields simultaneously without ever needing `self` as a whole.

prompt: I try to move a field out of a struct and also implement Drop on the struct. The compiler rejects the partial move.
error_message:
error[E0509]: cannot move out of type `Resource`, which implements the `Drop` trait
 --> src/main.rs:15:16
  |
15 |     let name = r.name;
   |                ^^^^^^
   |                |
   |                cannot move out of here
   |                move occurs because `r.name` has type `String`, which does not implement the `Copy` trait
explanation: E0509: when a type implements `Drop`, Rust forbids partial moves out of it. This is because `Drop::drop` receives `&mut self` and must be able to access all fields. The solution is to clone the field you want to use separately, or redesign so the struct is consumed in its entirety (e.g., via a consuming method that returns the fields after disabling the `Drop` via `mem::forget`, though that is rarely necessary).

prompt: I destructure a nested struct to move out an inner field, then try to use the outer struct again.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `o.inner.label`
 --> src/main.rs:15:22
  |
13 |     let label = o.inner.label;
   |                 ------------- value moved here
15 |     println!("{}", o.inner.label);
   |                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: Moving `o.inner.label` partially invalidates both `o.inner` and `o`. The field `o.count` can still be read (it is `Copy`) but `o` and `o.inner` cannot be used as wholes. Nested destructuring in one `let` binding handles all levels simultaneously without leaving any partially-moved intermediate.

prompt: I try to clone just one field of a struct instead of partial-moving it, but my attempt accidentally still moves the field.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `rec.name`
 --> src/main.rs:12:29
  |
9  |     let name_copy = rec.name;
   |                     -------- value moved here
12 |     println!("{:?}", (rec.id, rec.name));
   |                               ^^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: `rec.name` without `.clone()` is a move, not a copy. Adding `.clone()` creates a new independent `String` heap allocation, leaving `rec.name` intact. With that fix, `rec` is never partially moved and all fields remain accessible.

prompt: I try to call a method on a struct after having moved one of its Vec fields out in an earlier statement.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `p`
 --> src/main.rs:21:5
  |
20 |     let stages = p.stages;
   |                  -------- value partially moved here
21 |     p.run();
   |     ^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: Moving `p.stages` partially invalidates `p`; calling `p.run()` borrows `p` as a whole, which is disallowed after a partial move. The fix is to call `run()` first while `p` is still intact, then destructure `p` to extract `stages`.

prompt: I have a struct with three String fields. I move out two of them and then try to construct a new struct using the third field plus the moved ones — the compiler says the third field may have been moved.
explanation: Actually the original compiles fine in Rust 2021 because moving individual fields and then using the remaining field in a struct expression is permitted: `creds.token` is still valid after `creds.user` and `creds.pass` are moved. The idiomatic version nonetheless uses full destructuring to make intent clear and avoid relying on field-level tracking across statements.

prompt: I call `std::mem::replace` to swap a String field out of a struct, but then someone on my team accidentally tries to use the old partially-moved field.
explanation: The original code actually compiles: moving `s.message` partially moves `s`, but re-assigning `s.message` restores it to a fully-initialized state. However, `mem::replace` is the idiomatic tool for this swap pattern — it atomically takes the old value and inserts a new one through `&mut`, avoiding any partially-moved window and making intent explicit.

prompt: I move a field out of a struct in a for loop over a Vec of those structs, and the compiler says the struct is partially moved inside the loop.
error_message:
error[E0507]: cannot move out of `item.name` which is behind a shared reference
 --> src/main.rs:14:20
  |
14 |         let name = item.name;
   |                    ^^^^^^^^^
   |                    |
   |                    cannot move out of here
   |                    move occurs because `item.name` has type `String`, which does not implement the `Copy` trait
explanation: When iterating with `&items`, `item` is `&Item` — a reference. You cannot move a field out of a reference. Instead, borrow the field: `&item.name` or just use it directly in the format string. If you need an owned copy of the name, use `item.name.clone()`.

prompt: I have a struct update syntax (`..other`) expression but one of the fields I'm overriding was already moved out, causing a partial move in the base struct.
error_message:
error[E0382]: use of partially moved value: `defaults`
 --> src/main.rs:18:11
  |
15 |     let lang = defaults.language;
   |                ---------------- value partially moved here
18 |         ..defaults
   |           ^^^^^^^^ value used here after partial move
explanation: Struct update syntax `..defaults` moves all fields not explicitly listed out of `defaults`. But `defaults.language` was already moved, so `defaults` is partially moved and update syntax is rejected. The fix is to fully destructure `defaults` first, then build the new struct from the resulting bindings explicitly.

prompt: I move a String out of an Option<String> field of a struct, which partially moves the struct. Then I try to pattern match on the same Option field again.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `w.value`
 --> src/main.rs:13:44
  |
9  |     if let Some(v) = w.value {
   |                      ------- value partially moved here
13 |     println!("id: {}, has value: {}", w.id, w.value.is_some());
   |                                              ^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: `if let Some(v) = w.value` moves the inner `String` out of the `Option`, leaving `w.value` partially moved. Using `ref v` in the pattern borrows the inner string instead, leaving both `w.value` and `w` intact for subsequent use.

prompt: I want to split a struct into two halves by moving fields to two separate variables, but the compiler says the struct is used after it's partially moved.
explanation: Both fields are moved out, fully consuming `s`. After the two moves, `s` has no valid fields and cannot be used — not even for `Debug`. The idiomatic fix is full destructuring, which makes the intent clear. Print `left` and `right` directly instead of the consumed struct.

prompt: I try to access a struct field via an index expression after partially moving a different Vec field and the compiler rejects it.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `g`
 --> src/main.rs:13:20
  |
12 |     let rows = g.rows;
   |                ------ value partially moved here
13 |     println!("{} rows[0][0]={}", g.label, rows[0][0]);
   |                                  ^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: After `g.rows` is moved, `g` is partially moved. Even though `g.label` is still valid, borrowing `g` as a whole (which `g.label` implicitly does inside the macro) is rejected. Full destructuring in one step consumes `g` and provides `rows` and `label` as independent owned bindings.

prompt: I write a function that should consume a struct and return its two String fields separately, but I accidentally use `self` after the first field move.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `self.key`
 --> src/main.rs:11:36
  |
8  |         let k = self.key;
   |                 -------- value moved here
11 |         println!("splitting: {} {}", self.key, self.val);
   |                                      ^^^^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: After `self.key` is moved into `k`, any subsequent use of `self.key` (including in a borrow such as inside `println!`) is rejected. Destructuring `self` at the top of the function gives independent bindings for both fields at once, and those bindings can be used freely afterward.

prompt: I partially move a field out of a struct inside a closure and later try to use the struct again from the enclosing scope.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `d`
 --> src/main.rs:14:24
  |
9  |         let p = d.payload;
   |                 --------- value partially moved here
...
14 |     println!("id: {}", d.id);
   |                        ^^^^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: The closure captures `d` and moves `d.payload` out of it, partially invalidating `d`. After the closure, the outer scope tries to read `d.id`, which triggers the error. The fix is to copy `d.id` (a `Copy` type) before the closure is created, then let the `move` closure fully own `d`.

prompt: I have a struct with a Box<dyn Trait> field and a String field. I move the Box out and then try to call a method on the struct that uses the String field.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `s`
 --> src/main.rs:26:5
  |
25 |     let h = s.handler;
   |             --------- value partially moved here
26 |     s.log();
   |     ^ value borrowed here after partial move
explanation: Moving `s.handler` out before calling `s.log()` partially invalidates `s`. The fix is to call `log()` first while `s` is still whole, then destructure `s` to extract the handler. Field-level partial moves work, but any method call that borrows `&self` requires the struct to be fully intact.

prompt: I try to use struct update syntax to create a new struct, but one field of the base struct was already partially moved, so the spread fails.
error_message:
error[E0382]: use of partially moved value: `p`
 --> src/main.rs:12:23
  |
11 |     let label = p.label;
   |                 ------- value partially moved here
12 |     let q = Point { x: 5.0, ..p };
   |                              ^ value used here after partial move
explanation: After `p.label` is moved out, `p` is partially moved and struct update syntax `..p` is rejected. The solution is to not move `p.label` beforehand — instead, inline `p.label.clone()` directly in the struct literal so `p` remains intact for `..p` to copy `y` from. If you need `label` separately, clone it in the struct literal and keep the clone.

prompt: I have a Vec of structs and I move a field out of one element in the loop, causing a partial move error on the Vec.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `e.key`
 --> src/main.rs:16:39
  |
13 |         let k = e.key;
   |                 ----- value moved here
16 |         println!("whole key again: {}", e.key);
   |                                         ^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
explanation: Moving `e.key` into `k` partially moves `e` inside the loop body, so the subsequent use of `e.key` is invalid. Destructuring directly in the `for` pattern — `for Entry { key, score } in entries` — moves both fields at pattern-binding time and provides independent bindings with no partially-moved struct.

prompt: I want to compare two structs after moving a field from one of them into a function, but the whole-struct comparison fails due to partial move.
error_message:
error[E0382]: borrow of partially moved value: `r1`
 --> src/main.rs:17:25
  |
15 |     let len = use_name(r1.name);
   |                        ------- value partially moved here
17 |     println!("equal: {}", r1 == r2);
   |                           ^^ value used here after partial move
explanation: `use_name(r1.name)` moves the `name` field out of `r1`, partially invalidating it. The subsequent `r1 == r2` tries to borrow `r1` (for `PartialEq`), which is rejected. Changing `use_name` to borrow `&str` keeps `r1.name` intact, so the full comparison compiles.
